sources.name: TASVideos Game Resources — SNES / Mega Man X / Data
sources.notes: Physics constants confirmed: walk speed 1.5 px/f, dash speed 3.5 px/f, jump velocity 5.0 px/f, gravity 0.25 px/f², terminal velocity 5.75 px/f, ladder speed 1.5 px/f.

notes: Mega Man X is the SNES successor to the NES Mega Man series. Physics share core Capcom constants (gravity 0.25 px/f², walk 1.5 px/f) but extend significantly with dash and wall kick.
The dash (3.5 px/f) is the signature addition — 2.33x walk speed. Changes the movement vocabulary fundamentally.
Wall kick (wall jump) is the other major new mechanic. Absent from all NES Mega Man games.
Charge shot (introduced in NES MM4) is present and enhanced — three charge levels.
All primary physics values from TASVideos MMX Data page — high confidence.
The NES-to-SNES transition maintained the series' core feel (same gravity, same walk speed) while adding high-end movement tools.

nes_mm_comparison.jump_velocity: slightly higher: 5.0 px/f vs NES MM2 6.0 px/f... wait — NES MM2 is 6.0, MMX is 5.0. MMX jump is slightly lower.

subpixel_system.description: SNES MMX uses a fixed-point system similar to NES Mega Man. Exact sub-pixel unit per pixel requires ROM verification — values below are in px/frame.

walk_speed.notes: Identical to NES Mega Man series (1-6). Instant velocity — no acceleration ramp. Direction reversal is instant.

notes: Dash is the defining new movement mechanic in MMX. 3.5 / 1.5 = 2.33x walk speed.
Dash initiates immediately at full dash speed — no acceleration ramp.
Dash has a finite duration — automatically ends after a set number of frames.
Dash can be performed on ground or in air (air dash).
Dash → jump (dash jump) preserves dash momentum through the jump arc.

ground_movement.notes: Instant velocity on ground (same as NES series). No acceleration or deceleration curves for walking.

initial_y_velocity.notes: Standard jump velocity. Slightly lower than NES MM2 (6.0 px/f) but higher than NES MM3 (5.5 px/f).

slope_jump_bonus.notes: Jumping off an ascending slope gives a velocity bonus. Exact slope-angle-to-bonus mapping needs verification.

variable_jump.notes: Releasing jump early produces a shorter apex. Same variable-height mechanic as NES Mega Man series.

dash_jump.description: Jumping while dashing (or immediately after initiating dash) carries dash horizontal momentum through the jump arc.
notes: Dash jump covers dramatically more horizontal distance than a walk jump.
Ground dash → jump is the primary horizontal traversal tool for long gaps.
Air dash → the dash can also be performed in the air, giving a mid-air horizontal boost.

horizontal_speed_during_jump.notes: Horizontal speed during jump matches the speed at which the jump was initiated (walk or dash).

notes: Wall kick (wall jump) is a core mechanic in MMX — completely absent from all NES Mega Man games.
X slides down walls when airborne and in contact with a vertical surface.
Pressing jump while wall-sliding kicks X off the wall in the opposite horizontal direction.
Multiple wall kicks can chain to climb tall vertical shafts.
Wall kick + dash expands the vertical+horizontal reach of movement significantly.
Wall sliding speed: slower than normal fall (friction effect).

kick_y_velocity_approx.notes: Approximate — near standard jump velocity. Exact wall kick Y velocity needs ROM verification.

gravity.notes: Identical to NES Mega Man series. 0.25 px/frame² is the Capcom series constant for this era.

terminal_velocity.notes: Higher than NES Mega Man terminal velocity (4.0 px/f). SNES MMX falls faster at maximum.

notes: Standard ladder climb speed in MMX is 1.5 px/frame (same as walk speed).
In Mega Man X2 and X3, ladder climb speed increases to 3.0 px/f with a dash upgrade.
X can shoot horizontally from ladders.

charge_shot.notes: Charge shot is available from the start in MMX (no Arms Parts upgrade needed for base charge).

level_2.notes: Pierces some enemies and hits multiple times.

special_weapons.notes: X acquires a special weapon from each defeated Maverick boss. 8 special weapons total. Each has strengths/weaknesses against other bosses (type chart).

upgrades_system.description: MMX introduces armor upgrades: Helmet, Body, Arms (Arm Parts), Legs. Each found in a capsule hidden in a stage.
upgrades_system.helmet: Shows map information, breaks certain blocks with head slide
upgrades_system.arms_parts: Enhances charge shot to Level 2 (more powerful full charge)

legs.description: Enables air dash and double-tap dash on ground
legs.notes: Leg upgrade is usually the first obtained — enables air dash which dramatically expands movement options.

upgrades_system.heart_tanks: 8 total — each increases max HP by 2 units
upgrades_system.sub_tanks: 4 total — each stores up to 32 HP units for later use

full_armor_bonus.notes: Collecting all 4 armor parts grants a gold armor with additional enhancements beyond individual part effects.

standing.notes: Approximate — similar to NES Mega Man proportions. VERIFY against ROM.

dashing.notes: Dash hitbox may have slightly reduced height. Needs ROM verification.

wall_sliding.notes: Hitbox position adjusts when sliding on wall. X's sprite flips to face the wall.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Walk speed (1.5 px/f) and gravity (0.25 px/f²) are identical to NES Mega Man — the fundamental feel is preserved. The expansion is entirely in the ceiling: dash doubles range, wall kick opens vertical space, air dash adds a third dimension to movement.
Dash jump is the primary traversal tool — levels MUST have gaps that require dash jump to cross. Walk jump gaps feel like small obstacles; dash jump gaps are the real tests.

Wall kick transforms vertical space from one-directional (up via jump only) to bidirectional (ascend via wall chains, descend via gravity). Level designers can now use vertical height as real terrain.

The upgrade system creates a 'power curve' within a single playthrough: stages feel different with full armor. Design can account for this by having upgrades in early stages modify how hard stages feel later.
The weakness system (each boss weak to another boss's weapon) creates a meta-routing decision before the game starts. This is 'strategic' decision-making in a fast-action game — unusual design.
